explanations.4: This line calls a function _d_check_env to verify Docker is installed; if it fails (returns non-zero), the whole script stops and returns 1.
explanations.10: In Zsh, you can define a function inside a local scope using 'local' keyword, which keeps it private to this script block.
explanations.26: This loop processes command-line arguments one at a time; $# is the count of arguments, and shift removes the first one each iteration.
explanations.42: The awk command extracts the fifth column ($5) from the 'Build Cache' line in docker system df output; this gives us the cache size.
explanations.46: The ${(@f)variable} syntax splits the variable's contents by newlines into array elements; @f means 'split on field separator'.
explanations.70: This converts Docker image names to their short IDs (first 12 characters of the SHA256 hash) so they can be accurately compared and subtracted from the all_imgs array.
explanations.74: The ${all_imgs:|used_imgs} syntax is Zsh array subtraction—it removes any elements from all_imgs that appear in used_imgs, leaving only unused images.
explanations.96: This defines a nested function _fmt inside _draw_plan; it's a helper to format and color each step's status line consistently.
explanations.139: If force mode is on, this function immediately returns success (0) without prompting, so all cleanup steps auto-confirm.
explanations.140: The read -q flag makes the prompt non-blocking and case-insensitive; it returns 0 if the user presses 'y' or 'Y'.
explanations.171: The eval command executes the string stored in $img_cmd as if you'd typed it directly; this is needed because img_cmd contains the command to run.
explanations.208: This checks if buildx (Docker's newer builder system) is installed; if so, the script uses the newer buildx prune command instead of the older builder prune.
